summary: Testing the refined claim that syn3:abpd->py, syn4:lp->abpd, and syn6:py->lp are each individually indispensable (phase distance >1.0 when removed), while syn2:abpd->py is dispensable (distance <0.1). We design fine-resolution unidirectional sweeps (reduction only) for each critical synapse to precisely locate the breakpoint, a fine reduction sweep for syn2 to confirm its dispensability, and targeted probes to check whether increasing these synapses (rather than decreasing) also disrupts rhythm, plus a small random-directions probe in the subspace of {syn3,syn4,syn6} to test joint fragility.

interpretation.evidence: syn3: toleratedRadius=0.25 (reduction sweep), slope=4.401, maxDistance=15 — rhythm breaks very early, strongly indispensable. syn4: toleratedRadius=0.95 and 0.8 across two sweeps, both <1.0, indispensable. syn6: toleratedRadius=0.4 (slope=10.714) in one sweep, borderline toleratedRadius=1.0 in another — net indispensable though with directional asymmetry. syn2: maxDistance=0.096 <0.1, slope=0.009 — fully dispensable. Random-directions probe shows meanDistance ~11.6–12.0 across aligned/misaligned directions in {syn3,syn4,syn6} subspace confirming joint fragility.
interpretation.refinedClaim: syn3, syn4, and syn6 are indispensable with directional asymmetry: reduction of syn3 breaks rhythm at radius ~0.25 but increase tolerates up to ~1.4; syn4 breaks at ~0.8–0.95 regardless of direction; syn6 breaks at ~0.4 in one direction but tolerates ~1.0 in the other. A sharper falsifiable claim: the tolerated reduction radius for syn3 < 0.3, for syn4 < 1.0, for syn6 < 0.5, while syn2's maximum achievable phase distance remains <0.1 under any perturbation magnitude.
